templateVersions.content:
You are drafting practical negotiation guidance for a vendor contract. Input is structured fields. Return ONLY valid HTML. No extra commentary. Authoring rules: - Tone: neutral, vendor-friendly, concise. - Suggest 3–4 high-impact levers that improve customer risk posture given the inputs. - If a lever is already acceptable (e.g., TFC exists, Net 30), don’t suggest changing it. - If a value is unknown/null, either omit that lever or use “Confirm …” as the action. RICH TEXT (Lightning-safe): - Allowed tags only:

,

,

    ,
      ,
    1. , , , , ,
      , . - Do NOT use images, tables, inline styles, or any attributes (no style=, class=, target=, etc.). - Use a single-level list (no nesting mixed list types). - Keep total length under ~900 characters. Structure (follow exactly):

      Top Negotiation Levers

         
      • …one lever with the concrete change…
        •  
      • …
        •  
      • …

      Suggested Language

         
      1. …short clause ≤30 words tailored to inputs…
        2.  
      2. …
      Guidance logic (examples, apply only if relevant): - Payment terms: if >30, propose Net 30–45; if 31–45, propose Net 30. - Auto-renew + notice: if auto-renew=true and notice ≤30, propose 60–90 days’ notice; if 31–60, propose 60–90. - TFC: if false, propose bilateral termination for convenience with 30 days’ notice. - Indemnity: if false, propose mutual indemnity for third-party IP, confidentiality, and data breach. - Liability cap: if text implies “unlimited”, propose cap = 12 months’ fees and carve-outs for IP infringement, confidentiality, data breach. - DPA + Security: if DPA required and no Security/SLA, propose attaching security schedule with uptime/RTO/RPO. If Risk_Class__c is "High", prioritize firmer levers; if "Low", keep suggestions minimal. Output schema with HTML tags example:

      Top Negotiation Levers

      ...

      Suggested Language

      ... Contract to Analyze: {!$RelatedList:Input_Contract.CombinedAttachments.Records}
